petitioner_background: The petitioner is an environmental chemistry researcher who earned his Ph.D. in Environmental Sciences from Zhejiang University in 1999. At the time of filing (November 1, 2002), he was working as a postdoctoral research associate in the laboratory of Dr. Ann Lemley, Professor of Environmental Toxicology, at Cornell University. He previously worked in the research group of Dr. Jay Gan at the University of California, Riverside. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence that he was 'selected as runner-up winner of the 2002 ACS [American Chemical Society] Agrochemicals Division Young Scientist's Research Recognition Award.' Supporting information from the ACS website described it as an annual competition for Young Scientist Pre- and Post-doctoral Research Awards to recognize outstanding young agrochemical researchers. A letter from Dr. Allan Felsot, Professor at Washington State University and Chair of the ACS Division of Agrochemicals Young Scientist Research Recognition Award, stated that 8 applications were received during the petitioner's year, and the top two applicants received a small grant, travel stipend, complimentary ACS membership, and a plaque. The petitioner also submitted evidence that his Ph.D. dissertation was honored in 2002 by the Graduate Office of the Chinese Ministry of Education as one of one hundred 'Outstanding Doctorate Degree Dissertations' for the period from September 1999 to August 2000, along with a listing of all 100 Ph.D. dissertations selected.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that the petitioner was recognized not by outside nomination (demonstrating the field's regard) but upon his own application to the Agrochemical Division award committee. Competition for both awards was limited to recent Ph.D. graduates and post-doctoral researchers — awards presented not to established researchers with active professional careers, but to recent university graduates or those pursuing advanced scientific training in temporary research positions. The AAO stated it could not artificially restrict the petitioner's field to exclude established professional scientists who have completed their training and no longer compete for such age-limited honors. There was no indication the petitioner had received any significant award for which he would have faced competition from throughout his entire field, rather than his approximate age group within that field. The visa classification is intended for aliens already at the top of their fields, not individuals progressing toward the top at some unspecified future time.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a September 2, 2002 article in Chemical and Engineering News entitled 'Ridding the World of Unwanted Chemicals,' in which the petitioner's name is mentioned only twice and he was not among the scientists quoted; (2) three sentences about the petitioner's honor as runner-up of the 2002 ACS Agrochemicals Division Young Scientist's Research Recognition Award appearing on the 'Agrochemicals Division' portion of the ACS website; (3) a July 6, 2002 piece in China Education Daily listing the petitioner's name and dissertation as one of the 100 most outstanding Ph.D. dissertations; and (4) in response to the RFE, a review article and two research papers from 2003 that cite the petitioner's work.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found none of this material qualified. The Chemical and Engineering News article was not primarily about the petitioner — he was clearly not the primary subject. The three-sentence ACS website posting did not meet the regulatory requirement of 'published materials,' and more space was devoted to the first-place winner. The China Education Daily piece was about the overall competition and listed 99 other recipients in the same manner as the petitioner, so he was not the primary subject. The 2003 articles citing the petitioner's work came into existence subsequent to the filing date, and a petitioner must establish eligibility at the time of filing per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. Additionally, research papers citing the petitioner's work are primarily about the authors' own work, not the petitioner's work — such citations cannot be considered qualifying published material about the petitioner. Citations referenced numerous other authors and do not discuss the merits of an individual's work, their standing in the field, or significant impact. Citations would be addressed under a separate criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petition included correspondence from the Associate Editors of the Journal of Environmental Quality and the Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry showing the petitioner was requested to review a total of two manuscripts. These letters requested that if the petitioner could not complete the review, he pass it to a colleague or suggest another qualified reviewer. One letter was signed by Dr. Jay Gan, Assistant Professor at UC Riverside and Associate Editor for the Journal of Environmental Quality — who was the petitioner's former research mentor at UCR. In response to the RFE, the petitioner submitted correspondence from the Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry, Environmental Science & Technology, and the Journal of Environmental Quality indicating he reviewed manuscripts for these journals in 2003 and 2004. The petitioner's RFE response stated he had been invited to review 4 more papers after the November 2002 filing.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted the petitioner's close relationship with Dr. Gan (his former research mentor at UCR), concluding it could not find that the petitioner's notoriety as a top researcher — rather than his personal connection — resulted in his being selected as a reviewer for the Journal of Environmental Quality. The AAO observed that peer review is a routine element of the publication process; occasional participation in peer review does not automatically demonstrate sustained national or international acclaim at the very top of the field. Journals commonly enlist numerous professionals to review submitted papers. The post-filing evidence from 2003 and 2004 could not retroactively establish eligibility as of the filing date per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak. Without evidence setting the petitioner apart from others in his field as of the filing date — such as peer-reviewing an unusually large number of manuscripts, receiving multiple independent requests from a substantial number of journals, or serving in an editorial position for a distinguished journal (like Dr. Gan) — the AAO could not conclude he met this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted several letters of support. Dr. Ann Lemley (Cornell) stated the petitioner had been directly responsible for outstanding advances in her laboratory, developed a theoretical model describing degradation kinetics that was published in Environmental Science & Technology, and applied his physical chemistry background to pesticide degradation. Dr. Scott Yates (Professor of Soil Physics, UCR) stated the petitioner conducted novel research on the transformation of fumigants and chloroacetanilide herbicides by sodium thiosulfate, providing important information on reaction kinetics, mechanisms, and toxicity of byproducts. Dr. Jay Gan stated the petitioner's discovery on detoxification of halogenated contaminants by thiosulfate salts was potentially extremely useful for decontamination and pollution prevention, and implied the petitioner contributed to a U.S. patent. Counsel also asserted the petitioner's UCR work resulted in U.S. Patent No. 5,904,909. Dr. Allan Felsot (Washington State University) stated the petitioner's collaboration with Dr. Lemley led to an extraordinarily novel and 'green' method for degrading liquid pesticide waste, brilliantly modifying an old waste degradation method. Dr. Chin-Pao Huang (Distinguished Professor of Environmental Engineering, University of Delaware) discussed the petitioner's Fenton technology paper and a kinetic model for the anodic Fenton process published in Environmental Science & Technology, and his work on weak interaction between triazine/trizinon herbicides and ferric ion. Dr. Aldos Barefoot (Research Fellow at DuPont Crop Protection) also discussed the petitioner's publication record.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the documentation inadequate. The record contained no evidence showing the petitioner's method was widely utilized or hailed as a major achievement throughout the environmental remediation industry. The petitioner's work added to the overall body of knowledge, but this is the goal of all research; assertions of potential practical applications do not distinguish him from competent researchers. Critically, the AAO found the assertion that the petitioner contributed to U.S. Patent No. 5,904,909 was 'a blatant attempt to misrepresent the facts' — U.S. Patent and Trademark Office records show Professors Yates and Gan were the inventors of this patent, which was filed August 21, 1997, long before the petitioner entered the U.S. or worked in their group. Per Matter of Ho, 19 I&N Dec. 582, the petitioner must resolve inconsistencies with competent objective evidence. No evidence showed the petitioner held a patent for the 'green' degradation method or that it had been widely implemented. The AAO noted that publication falls under a separate criterion and the criteria are separate and distinct — evidence sufficient for one criterion cannot automatically satisfy another. Most letters were from immediate colleagues or professional acquaintances, indicating the petitioner's work is valued by those close to him while others outside his immediate circle are largely unaware. Personal recommendations from the petitioner's affiliated institutions are not first-hand evidence of sustained acclaim outside those institutions; an individual with sustained acclaim should produce ample unsolicited materials reflecting that acclaim.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of authorship of articles appearing in publications such as Environmental Science & Technology, Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry, Environmental Toxicology and Chemistry, and Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the U.S.A. The petitioner also provided scientific citation indices showing that his research articles are frequently cited.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO held that publication of scholarly articles is not presumptive evidence of sustained national or international acclaim; the greater field's reaction to those articles must also be considered. When judging influence and impact, the act of publication is not as reliable a gauge as the citation history. Publication alone may serve as evidence of originality, but it is difficult to conclude a published article is important or influential without evidence that other researchers have relied upon the findings. Based on the frequent citation of his published work as shown in scientific citation indices, the AAO found the evidence adequate to satisfy this criterion.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO found the petitioner satisfied only one of the ten regulatory criteria (scholarly articles), falling short of the required three. The fundamental nature of this highly restrictive visa classification demands comparison between the petitioner and others in his field, and a simple comparison with Dr. Lemley and Dr. Yates showed their level of professional accomplishment far exceeded the petitioner's. The AAO emphasized the classification is reserved for those already at the top of their field, not those who are expected eventually to reach that level — however respected the petitioner may be and whatever future promise his career may hold, he had not yet reached the very top. The evidence was not persuasive that the petitioner's achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at the national or international level, so he failed to establish sustained national or international acclaim necessary under section 203(b)(1)(A). The burden of proof remained with the petitioner, who did not sustain it, and the appeal was dismissed.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The AAO explicitly found that the assertion by counsel and Dr. Jay Gan that the petitioner contributed to U.S. Patent No. 5,904,909 was 'a blatant attempt to misrepresent the facts of this case.' According to U.S. Patent and Trademark Office records, Professors Yates and Gan were the actual inventors of the patent 'Methods for Removing and Decomposing Methyl Bromide from Fumigation,' which was filed on August 21, 1997 — long before the petitioner entered the U.S. or worked in their research group at UCR. The AAO cited Matter of Ho, 19 I&N Dec. 582, 591-92, noting that the petitioner bears the burden of resolving inconsistencies in the record with independent objective evidence. Additionally, the AAO noted that significant portions of the petitioner's evidence (2003 citing articles and 2003-2004 peer review requests) came into existence after the November 1, 2002 filing date and could not retroactively establish eligibility per 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(12) and Matter of Katigbak.
